p: NidamJS is a framework-agnostic JavaScript library designed to inject desktop-like logic into web frontends. It enables windows, taskbar, icons, and related UI/UX features without requiring backend changes.
Window Management: Full window lifecycle (open, close, minimize, maximize, snapping).
Desktop Experience: Taskbar, desktop icons, and windows.
Framework Agnostic: Works with React, Vue, Svelte, Angular, or Vanilla JS.
Detailed docs on Windows, Taskbar, and Icons.
NidamJS is released under the MIT License.
Get started with NidamJS by adding it to your project. You can install it via your favorite package manager or include it directly via CDN.
Install NidamJS using npm or bun.
Now that you have installed NidamJS, head over to the Quick Start guide to create your first desktop-like environment.

Getting started with NidamJS is quick and flexible. Depending on your needs, you can either drop in the script for automatic initialization with default settings or take full manual control to pass a custom configuration object.
Required: Add the NidamJS script, link, and div nd-desktop to your page.
Everything else inside div nd-desktop is optional — add only the components you need (icons, taskbar, toasts).

c: Hello

p: Icons are the primary interactive elements of the NidamJS desktop. They represent applications, files, or system actions. The system uses a declarative, attribute-based grid that handles positioning, drag-and-drop, and layout persistence automatically.
To create a functional desktop icon grid, wrap your icons in a container using the nd-icons attribute.

c:
Web Browser
Trash

p: The icon system is controlled via three main nd- attributes. You generally do not need to write JavaScript to manage these; the internal IconManager detects them automatically.
Applied to the parent element to define the grid dimensions.
Example: nd-icons=8:5 creates a grid with 8 columns and 5 rows.
Applied to individual icon elements to set their initial or current position.
Behavior: If a position is already occupied, the system will automatically find the nearest available spot.
Required for persistence. * Purpose: This ID is used as the key when saving the icon's position to local storage. Without an nd-id, the icon will reset to its default HTML position on page refresh.
Defines the grid size (e.g., 10:6). Sets CSS variables --nd-cols and --nd-rows.
The ID of the window/modal to trigger on click.
The X:Y coordinate of the icon. Updates dynamically after a drag.
Unique string used to save/load the position from storage.
Deadzone & Delay: A small movement "deadzone" and time delay prevent accidental drags during simple clicks.
Ghosting: While dragging, a transparent "ghost" element follows the cursor (.nd-icon-ghost), while the original icon remains dimmed (.is-dragging).
Auto-Snapping: Upon release, the icon snaps to the nearest valid grid cell.
Collision Detection: Icons cannot overlap. If you drop an icon on an occupied cell, the move is canceled.
The layout is automatically saved to localStorage under the key nd-icons-layout.
On page load, the system checks storage for coordinates matching the icon's nd-id.
If found, the nd-icon attribute is updated, and the icon is moved to its last saved position.
NidamJS icons are built with Design Tokens (CSS Variables). You can easily customize the entire desktop experience by overriding these variables in your own stylesheet.
All variables are defined under :root but can be scoped to specific [nd-icons] containers if you want different styles for different desktop areas.
Inner padding of the desktop container.
Responsive sizing of the icon image.
The background color of the icon text.
The scale factor when hovering an icon.
Transparency of the icon following the mouse.
Transparency of the original icon during drag.
The system automatically adapts to prefers-color-scheme: dark, but you can force specific colors:
If you want a dense, small-icon grid, you can override the variables directly on the container:
While you should primarily use variables, you can target these classes for advanced CSS overrides:
.is-dragging: Added to the icon currently being moved.
.nd-icon-ghost: The temporary element created during a drag operation (appended to body).
Note: The nd-icon-ghost element is removed from the DOM as soon as the mouse is released.

The Taskbar component provides a persistent interface for launching applications and managing open windows in the NidamJS desktop environment. It displays application icons that serve as triggers to open windows, with visual feedback when windows are open.

The taskbar acts as the primary navigation hub for the desktop. It docks to one edge of the screen and displays application icons. When a window is open, the corresponding taskbar icon shows an active indicator, and clicking the icon will either bring the existing window into focus or open a new one.
The TaskbarManager class handles the taskbar functionality:
Event Binding: Listens for clicks on taskbar icons to trigger window opening
Window State Sync: Subscribes to window:opened and window:closed events to update icon states
Visual Feedback: Toggles the is-open class on icons based on window state

When you click a taskbar icon, the manager calls windowManager.open(endpoint) with the endpoint specified in the icon's data-modal attribute. If a window with that endpoint is already open, it will be brought into focus.
Flexible Positioning: Dock the taskbar to the bottom (default), left, or right side of the screen
Visual Indicators: Active applications display a pill-shaped indicator below the icon
Automatic Sync: Real-time updates based on window lifecycle events
Responsive Animations: Smooth transitions for hover states and state changes
Multiple Styles: Default floating style and extended Windows-style bar
The taskbar is defined in HTML using the nd-taskbar attribute. Individual items use the nd-taskbar-icon attribute and must map to a window endpoint via data-modal.
NidamJS provides two built-in taskbar styles:
The default floating style positions the taskbar as a rounded bar that floats above the desktop with a glassmorphic appearance.
The extended style creates a full-width bar docked to the bottom of the screen, similar to the Windows taskbar.
You can change the orientation and position of the taskbar using the nd-taskbar-position attribute.
For the extended taskbar style, you can add text labels beneath or beside icons:
The TaskbarManager targets specific attributes to apply logic and styling.
The taskbar styles are organized in three CSS files:
root.css: Defines all CSS custom properties (variables)
default.css: Implements the default floating taskbar style
extend.css: Implements the extended Windows-style taskbar
Customize the look and feel of the taskbar by overriding CSS custom properties in your stylesheet. Variables are scoped under the [nd-taskbar] selector.
When using nd-taskbar=extend, additional variables customize the Windows-style appearance:
Sets the placement of the taskbar.
The window endpoint/ID to trigger on click.
The TaskbarManager listens for these window events:
Fired when a window opens. Updates the corresponding taskbar icon to show the active indicator.
Fired when a window closes. Removes the active indicator from the corresponding taskbar icon.
To manually control taskbar icons from JavaScript:
NidamJS provides a flexible and easy-to-use Toast notification system. It allows you to display temporary messages to the user, with customizable positions, durations, and severity levels.
To use toasts, you must include the toast stack container in your HTML. Without this, NidamJS will log a warning and won't know where to render the toasts.
You can then import and call the toast functions:
The toast system relies on a few data attributes to manage the display and positioning of notifications.
Applied to an empty div, this acts as the anchor for all incoming notifications.
Defines the default position for all toasts within the stack.
The core functionality is exposed via JavaScript exports.
Auto-dismiss timeout in milliseconds. Set to 0 or less to disable auto-dismissal.
Customization options for this specific toast.
If a String is provided, a standard text toast is displayed.
If an Array of strings is provided, it will render them as a bulleted list (ul).
If an Object is provided, it automatically extracts message values (or an .errors property array if one is present).
Override the stack position for this specific toast (e.g., top-left).
The core function used to display a toast notification.
The message(s) to display. Intelligently parses multiple structures.
The severity of the toast (info, success, error, warning). Defaults to info if invalid.
Whether to render the close (x) button.
Identifies the main toast stacking element.
The default corner to stack toasts.
Toasts use the .nd-toast class, with the data-type attribute representing their visual states. You can override styles using regular CSS.
State-based styling variations (success, error, warning, info).
Unordered list styling for multiple messages.

Windows are the core containers of the NidamJS desktop environment. They provide draggable, focusable, resizable, tileable, and dynamically loaded UI panels — fully managed by the internal WindowManager.
The system is declarative and attribute-driven, requiring little to no manual JavaScript wiring.
A basic window structure looks like this:
The WindowManager automatically detects the data-modal attribute and loads the window content.

c: Page One

p: The window system is powered by declarative nd- attributes.
Applied to the root element of a window.
Marks the element as a managed window
If no size is specified, the default is 768x480.
Required for dynamic loading & lifecycle management.
Used internally as the unique key in the WindowManager.
Dragging starts only from this element.
Buttons must live inside the header.
Padding and styling are applied automatically.
Clicking a window brings it to the front.
The focused window receives the .focused class.
Automatically restores from maximized when dragging
Saves position ratios for responsive resizing
Corners → Quadrant tiling (if enabled in config)
A .snap-indicator overlay appears to preview placement.
Marks the element as a window
Like Icons, Windows use Design Tokens (CSS Variables).
You typically do not interact with these directly — use the public API:
✔ Declarative HTML structure ✔ Built-in drag & snap ✔ Focus & stacking management ✔ Dynamic content loading ✔ Persistent responsive positioning ✔ Design token theming
It behaves like a real desktop OS — inside the browser.
NidamJS exposes a carefully curated set of public functions and classes designed for interacting with its windowing system, desktop icons, and event delegation.
Initializes the NidamApp with an optional configuration object and returns the fully initialized app instance.
Note: NidamJS will automatically call initNidamApp() on script load unless you add a script tag with the [data-nd-init] attribute to your document to signify manual initialization.
config (Object | string) (Optional): The NidamJS configuration object or JSON string.
The main instance returned by initialization. It coordinates all modules in the NidamJS ecosystem.
Retrieves a specific initialized module by its registered name. This is the primary way to programmatically interact with NidamJS at runtime.
name (string): The name of the module. Valid module names are window, delegator, taskbar, refresher, and icon.
The requested module instance, or undefined if not found.
Mapstring, object: A JavaScript Map containing all available module instances.
After initializing NidamJS, you can access the core modules to manually trigger actions like opening windows, refreshing content, or listening to global events.
Controls the lifecycle, focus, and state of windows/modals. Accessible via app.getModule(window).

open(endpoint, force = false, focusSelector = null, activate = true) Opens a new window based on its endpoint or focuses an existing one. Returns a Promise tracking the DOM injection and initialization.
close(winElement) Closes the specified window element. Triggered automatically by GUI buttons.
closeTopmost() Closes the active (topmost/focused) window. Often mapped internally to the "Escape" key behavior.
getWindows() Returns an array of [endpoint, winElement] pairs representing all currently open windows.
focus(winElement) Brings the specified window to the front by updating its z-index position.
toggleMaximize(winElement) Toggles the given window between its maximized state and its previous restored bounds.
drag(e, winElement) Manually triggers a window drag operation, consuming a MouseEvent.
Handles centralized root-level HTML event delegation (like click, mousedown, keydown).
on(eventType, selector, handler, options = { group }) Registers a delegated event handler on the app's root. If selector is null, it works as a global handler. Returns a handler reference object.
off(eventType, ref) Removes a previously registered event handler using the reference object returned by .on().
Synchronizes the HTML taskbar layer functionality and icon rendering with the currently open modal instances. Interaction is primarily automatic across $window:opened and $window:closed events.
Controls desktop icon grid positioning constraints, snapping, and layout memory functionality on the DOM [nd-icons] container.
NidamJS is fully configurable through a structured configuration object.
To use a custom configuration, the script must be loaded manually.
Defines where event delegation is attached.
Use this if you want multiple isolated desktops.
CSS selector where windows are injected.
Dataset key used internally to mark elements that are waiting to open.
Only override if integrating with another system.
Used for advanced content injection patterns.
Increase if integrating with high z-index UI systems.
Wait time before layout calculations after content render.
Increase if using heavy async rendering frameworks.
Minimum time before reopening the same endpoint.
Distance from viewport edge required to trigger snap.
Minimum cursor travel (px) before drag starts.
Debounce delay for window resize calculations.
Should match your CSS animation durations.
Fallback width when no preset is defined.
Fallback height when no preset is defined.
Minimum distance between window and viewport edge.
Prevents windows from being partially off-screen.
Defines how much of viewport edges detect side snaps.
0.4 → 40% of vertical edge area detects left/right snap.
Maximum wait time for scroll restoration after async content load.
Increase if large content loads slowly.

c:
windowManager: {
  zIndexBase: 40,
  layoutStabilizationMs: 450,
  cascadeOffset: 30,
  cooldownMs: 500,
  maxWindows: 10,
  snapGap: 6,
  taskbarHeight: 64,
  snapThreshold: 30,
  dragThreshold: 10,
  resizeDebounceMs: 6,
  animationDurationMs: 400,
  defaultWidth: 800,
  defaultHeight: 600,
  minMargin: 10,
  edgeDetectionRatio: 0.4,
  scrollRestoreTimeoutMs: 2000
}
